texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Proofs that the Gerber Statistic is Positive Semidefinite
texts.text: Proofs that the Gerber Statistic is Positive Semidefinite

texts.label: text
texts.orig: S. Gerber, H. Markowitz, P.A. Ernst, Y. Miao, B. Javid, and P. Sargen
texts.text: S. Gerber, H. Markowitz, P.A. Ernst, Y. Miao, B. Javid, and P. Sargen

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Abstract
texts.text: Abstract

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this brief note, we prove that both forms of the Gerber statistic introduced in [1] are positive semi-definite.
texts.text: In this brief note, we prove that both forms of the Gerber statistic introduced in [1] are positive semi-definite.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 1 Theory
texts.text: 1 Theory

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We first recall the background theory of the Gerber statistic, as in [1]. Let r tk be the return of security k at time t, for k = 1 , . . . , K securities and t = 1 , . . . , T time periods. For every pair ( i, j ) of assets for each time t , we convert each return observation pair ( r ti , r tj ) to a joint observation m ij ( t ) defined as:
texts.text: We first recall the background theory of the Gerber statistic, as in [1]. Let r tk be the return of security k at time t, for k = 1 , . . . , K securities and t = 1 , . . . , T time periods. For every pair ( i, j ) of assets for each time t , we convert each return observation pair ( r ti , r tj ) to a joint observation m ij ( t ) defined as:

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: m ij ( t ) =                 +1 if r ti ≥ + H i and r tj ≥ + H j , +1 if r ti ≤ -H i and r tj ≤ -H j , -1 if r ti ≥ + H i and r tj ≤ -H j , -1 if r ti ≤ -H i and r tj ≥ + H j , 0 otherwise,

texts.label: text
texts.orig:  where H k is a threshold for security k. The joint observation m ij ( t ) is therefore set to +1 if the series i and j simultaneously pierce their thresholds in the same direction at time t, to -1 if they pierce their thresholds in opposite directions at time t , or to zero if at least one of the series does not pierce its threshold at time t . We refer to a pair for which both components pierce their thresholds while moving in the same direction as a concordant pair, and to one whose components pierce their thresholds while moving in opposite directions as a discordant pair. We set the threshold H k for security k to be
texts.text:  where H k is a threshold for security k. The joint observation m ij ( t ) is therefore set to +1 if the series i and j simultaneously pierce their thresholds in the same direction at time t, to -1 if they pierce their thresholds in opposite directions at time t , or to zero if at least one of the series does not pierce its threshold at time t . We refer to a pair for which both components pierce their thresholds while moving in the same direction as a concordant pair, and to one whose components pierce their thresholds while moving in opposite directions as a discordant pair. We set the threshold H k for security k to be

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: H k = cσ k

texts.label: text
texts.orig: where c is some fraction (say 1 / 2) and σ k is the sample standard deviation of the return of security k.
texts.text: where c is some fraction (say 1 / 2) and σ k is the sample standard deviation of the return of security k.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The Gerber statistic for a pair of assets is then initially defined as
texts.text: The Gerber statistic for a pair of assets is then initially defined as

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: g ij = ∑ T t =1 m ij ( t ) ∑ T t =1 ∣ ∣ m ij ( t ) ∣ ∣ . (1) 1

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Letting n c ij be the number of concordant pairs for series i and j , and letting n d ij be the number of discordant pairs, equation (1) is equivalent to
texts.text: Letting n c ij be the number of concordant pairs for series i and j , and letting n d ij be the number of discordant pairs, equation (1) is equivalent to

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: g ij = n c ij -n d ij n c ij + n d ij .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Let us define R ∈ R T × K to be the return matrix having r tk in its t -th row and k -th column. We may also define U as a matrix with the same size as R having entries u tj such that
texts.text: Let us define R ∈ R T × K to be the return matrix having r tk in its t -th row and k -th column. We may also define U as a matrix with the same size as R having entries u tj such that

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: u tj = { 1 if r tj ≥ + H j , 0 otherwise.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: With this definition, the matrix of the number of samples that exceed the upper threshold is
texts.text: With this definition, the matrix of the number of samples that exceed the upper threshold is

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: N UU = U ⊤ U .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Specifically, we have the useful property that the ij element n UU ij of N UU is the number of samples for which both time series i exceeds the upper threshold and for which time series j simultaneously exceeds the upper threshold.
texts.text: Specifically, we have the useful property that the ij element n UU ij of N UU is the number of samples for which both time series i exceeds the upper threshold and for which time series j simultaneously exceeds the upper threshold.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Similarly, define D as the matrix with the same size as R having entries d tj such that
texts.text: Similarly, define D as the matrix with the same size as R having entries d tj such that

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: d tj = { 1 if r tj ≤ -H j , 0 otherwise.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: With this definition, the matrix of the number of samples that go below the lower threshold is
texts.text: With this definition, the matrix of the number of samples that go below the lower threshold is

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: N DD = D ⊤ D .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Again, we have the useful property that the ij element n DD ij of N DD is the number of samples for which both time series i goes below the lower threshold and for which time series j simultaneously goes below the lower threshold. The matrix containing the numbers of concordant pairs is therefore
texts.text: Again, we have the useful property that the ij element n DD ij of N DD is the number of samples for which both time series i goes below the lower threshold and for which time series j simultaneously goes below the lower threshold. The matrix containing the numbers of concordant pairs is therefore

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: N CONC = N UU + N DD = U ⊤ U + D ⊤ D .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: It can be shown that the matrix containing the numbers of discordant pairs is
texts.text: It can be shown that the matrix containing the numbers of discordant pairs is

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: N DISC = U ⊤ D + D ⊤ U .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We can now write the Gerber matrix G (i.e., the matrix that contains g ij in its i -th row and j -th column) in the equivalent matrix form
texts.text: We can now write the Gerber matrix G (i.e., the matrix that contains g ij in its i -th row and j -th column) in the equivalent matrix form

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: G = ( N CONC -N DISC ) ⊘ ( N CONC + N DISC )

texts.label: text
texts.orig: where the symbol ⊘ represents the Hadamard (elementwise) division.
texts.text: where the symbol ⊘ represents the Hadamard (elementwise) division.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 1.1 A Graphical Relationship
texts.text: 1.1 A Graphical Relationship

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To simplify the description of various versions of the Gerber statistic, it is useful to consider the following graphical representation for the relationship between two securities:
texts.text: To simplify the description of various versions of the Gerber statistic, it is useful to consider the following graphical representation for the relationship between two securities:

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: UD UN UU ND NN NU DD DN DU

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Let the rows here represent categorizations of security i, and let the columns represent categorizations of security j. The boundaries between the rows and the columns are the chosen thresholds. The letter U represents the case in which a security's return lies above the upper threshold (i.e., is up). The letter N represents the case in which a security's return lies between the upper and lower thresholds (i.e., is neutral). The letter D represents the case in which a security's return lies below the lower threshold (i.e., is down).
texts.text: Let the rows here represent categorizations of security i, and let the columns represent categorizations of security j. The boundaries between the rows and the columns are the chosen thresholds. The letter U represents the case in which a security's return lies above the upper threshold (i.e., is up). The letter N represents the case in which a security's return lies between the upper and lower thresholds (i.e., is neutral). The letter D represents the case in which a security's return lies below the lower threshold (i.e., is down).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For example, if at time t, the return of security i is above the upper threshold, this observation lies in the top row. If, at the same time t, the return of security j lies between the two thresholds, this observation lies in the middle column. Therefore, this observation lies in the UN region. Over the history, t = 1 , . . . , T, there will be observations scattered over the nine regions. Let n pq ij be the number of observations for which the returns of securities i and j lie in regions p and q, respectively, for p, q ∈ { U, N, D } .
texts.text: For example, if at time t, the return of security i is above the upper threshold, this observation lies in the top row. If, at the same time t, the return of security j lies between the two thresholds, this observation lies in the middle column. Therefore, this observation lies in the UN region. Over the history, t = 1 , . . . , T, there will be observations scattered over the nine regions. Let n pq ij be the number of observations for which the returns of securities i and j lie in regions p and q, respectively, for p, q ∈ { U, N, D } .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The covariance matrix constructed from the 'initial' Gerber statistic as defined in equation (1) was often not positive semidefinite (PSD). We therefore tested some alternative versions that were PSD. Two alternative versions are as follows. It is important to note that in [1], we employed 'Gerber Statistic 2.'
texts.text: The covariance matrix constructed from the 'initial' Gerber statistic as defined in equation (1) was often not positive semidefinite (PSD). We therefore tested some alternative versions that were PSD. Two alternative versions are as follows. It is important to note that in [1], we employed 'Gerber Statistic 2.'

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Gerber Statistic 1 : Let the matrix G (1) be defined as containing g (1) ij in its i th row and j th column, with
texts.text: Gerber Statistic 1 : Let the matrix G (1) be defined as containing g (1) ij in its i th row and j th column, with

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: n ( A ) ij = n UU ij + n UN ij + n UD ij + n DU ij + n DN ij + n DD ij n ( B ) ij = n UU ij + n NU ij + n UD ij + n UD ij + n ND ij + n DD ij .

texts.label: text

texts.text: Gerber Statistic 2 : Let the matrix G (2) be defined as containing g (2) ij in its i th row and j th column, with Let us consider

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Let H = N CONC -N DISC , and let h = √ diag( H ) be the vector of square roots of the diagonal elements of H (which are all positive). We now see that the first alternative version of the Gerber statistic can be written in the matrix form
texts.text: Let H = N CONC -N DISC , and let h = √ diag( H ) be the vector of square roots of the diagonal elements of H (which are all positive). We now see that the first alternative version of the Gerber statistic can be written in the matrix form

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: G (1) = ( N CONC -N DISC ) ⊘ ( hh ⊤ ) .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Written differently, letting J = J ⊤ be the diagonal matrix with the inverse of the i -th element of h in its i -th diagonal position, we have
texts.text: Written differently, letting J = J ⊤ be the diagonal matrix with the inverse of the i -th element of h in its i -th diagonal position, we have

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: G (1) = J ⊤ ( N CONC -N DISC ) J .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Portfolio optimizers require the covariance matrix of securities' returns to be positive semidefinite. We therefore prove that both matrices G (1) and G (2) are positive semidefinite.
texts.text: Portfolio optimizers require the covariance matrix of securities' returns to be positive semidefinite. We therefore prove that both matrices G (1) and G (2) are positive semidefinite.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2 Gerber Statistic 1
texts.text: 2 Gerber Statistic 1

texts.label: text
texts.orig: First, it is useful to note that the numerator matrices of both G (1) and G (2) are positive semidefinite. We can see this as follows. From the definitions of N CONC and N DISC , the numerator matrix can be written in the following squared form:
texts.text: First, it is useful to note that the numerator matrices of both G (1) and G (2) are positive semidefinite. We can see this as follows. From the definitions of N CONC and N DISC , the numerator matrix can be written in the following squared form:

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: H = N CONC -N DISC = U ⊤ U + D ⊤ D -U ⊤ D -D ⊤ U = ( U -D ) ⊤ ( U -D ) .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Therefore, for arbitrary but non-zero x ,
texts.text: Therefore, for arbitrary but non-zero x ,

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: x ⊤ Hx = x ⊤ ( U -D ) ⊤ ( U -D ) x = u ⊤ u ≥ 0 .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We now prove that the matrix G (1) is positive semidefinite.
texts.text: We now prove that the matrix G (1) is positive semidefinite.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Proposition 1. The matrix G (1) is positive semidefinite.
texts.text: Proposition 1. The matrix G (1) is positive semidefinite.

texts.orig: This concludes the proof.
texts.text: This concludes the proof.
